title: The Giroux Correspondence in Arbitrary Dimensions
speaker: Ko Honda
abstract: Around twenty years ago Emmanuel Giroux formulated the equivalence of contact structures and open book decompositions with Weinstein pages up to stabilization. We establish the Giroux correspondence in full generality using the recent developments in convex hypersurface theory. This is joint work with Joe Breen and Yang Huang.

proof_stages.label: Giroux correspondence framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the arbitrary-dimensional Giroux correspondence by defining supported open books with Liouville/Weinstein pages, positive stabilization by a Dehn twist after attaching an n-handle, and the target theorem that every contact manifold has a strongly Weinstein open book and any two such open books are stably equivalent.

proof_stages.label: Convex hypersurface and bypass toolkit
proof_stages.summary: Convex hypersurfaces are introduced through transverse contact vector fields, dividing sets, positive and negative Liouville regions, characteristic foliations, Weinstein convexity, and the key theorems that hypersurfaces can be C⁰-approximated by Weinstein convex ones and one-parameter families fail convexity only by bypass attachments.

proof_stages.label: Morse-plus dynamics and contact plugs
proof_stages.summary: The characteristic foliation is analyzed Morse-theoretically: singularities have restricted positive/negative indices, retrogradient trajectories are the obstruction to convexity, k-Morse-plus hypersurfaces are convex, and mushroom-type contact plugs are used to block unwanted dynamics while keeping the perturbation C⁰-small.

proof_stages.label: Construction of open books from theta decompositions
proof_stages.summary: Using contact handlebodies modeled on Weinstein domains and Gromov’s h-principle, a Morse splitting of the contact manifold is converted into a theta or mushroom-burger decomposition whose bypass handles can be redistributed to the two sides, producing the two contact handlebodies and common boundary data of an open book.

proof_stages.label: Two-parameter bypass calculus for stable equivalence
proof_stages.summary: Stable equivalence is reduced to two-parametric convex hypersurface theory: generic families of contact structures are made two-Morse, all possible retrogradient bifurcations are enumerated, and each change in a bypass sequence is shown to be either far commutativity or insertion of a trivial bypass, the latter corresponding to stabilization.

proof_stages.label: Flexibilization of contact handlebodies
proof_stages.summary: To move the skeletons of the contact handlebodies freely during the comparison, the handlebodies are made flexible via wrinkled stabilization/unfurled swallowtail models, and these local modifications are related back to ordinary bypass operations.

proof_stages.label: Birth-death handle comparison
proof_stages.summary: The final ingredient treats a one-parameter Morse birth-death of a smoothly cancelling index n and n+1 pair by approximating the smooth attaching data by Legendrian/Lagrangian data, constructing matched sweepouts on the two sides, and checking that the resulting bypasses and n-handle skeletons agree up to the controlled extra stabilizing pieces.

examples.name: Liouville pages with high homology
examples.note: A general Liouville open book whose page has topology above the middle dimension illustrates why stable equivalence to a Weinstein open book can fail, since stabilization only changes middle-dimensional data.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses Liouville pages with homology above the middle dimension to argue that stabilization only changes middle-dimensional topology and therefore cannot make them equivalent to Weinstein pages

examples.name: Three-dimensional characteristic foliations
examples.note: In dimension three the characteristic foliation is simply ξ∩TΣ, a familiar special case used to contrast the extra symplectic-kernel condition in higher dimensions.
examples.engagement_reason: he specializes the characteristic foliation definition to dimension three and reasons through the line field, retrogradient trajectories, and plug behavior in that setting

examples.name: Giroux’s S² bifurcation prototype
examples.note: Giroux’s dimension-three bifurcation analysis for an S² in a contact manifold is recalled as a prototype whose older dynamical formulation did not extend well to more complicated surfaces.
examples.engagement_reason: he cites Giroux’s S² bifurcation prototype and explains its limitation to S², but does not analyze the prototype itself

examples.name: Dimension-three Legendrian arc and convex disk model
